subject: ap-chemistry
title: Metal-Air Electrochemical Cells
stimulus:
Metal-air cells are a relatively new type of portable energy source consisting of a metal anode, an alkaline
electrolyte paste that contains water, and a porous cathode membrane that lets in oxygen from the air. A
schematic of the cell is shown above. Reduction potentials for the cathode and three possible metal anodes are
given in the table below.
Half Reaction E at pH 11 and 298 K (V)
$O_2$(g) + 2 $H_2O$(l) + 4 e$^-$ ŌåÆ 4 OH$^-$(aq) +0.34
ZnO(s) + $H_2O$(l) + 2 e$^-$ ŌåÆ Zn(s) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) ŌĆō1.31
$Na_2O$(s) + $H_2O$(l) + 2 e$^-$ ŌåÆ 2 Na(s) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) ŌĆō1.60
CaO(s) + $H_2O$(l) + 2 e$^-$ ŌåÆ Ca(s) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) ŌĆō2.78

parts.prompt:
Early forms of metal-air cells used zinc as the anode. Zinc oxide is produced as the cell operates according
to the overall equation below.
2 Zn(s) + $O_2$(g) ŌåÆ 2 ZnO(s)
(i) Using the data in the table above, calculate the cell potential for the zinc-air cell.
(ii) The electrolyte paste contains OH$^-$ ions. On the diagram of the cell above, draw an arrow to indicate
the direction of migration of OH$^-$ ions through the electrolyte as the cell operates.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): The cathode is O$_2$ reduction (+0.34 V) and the anode is Zn oxidation (reverse of ZnO/Zn half-reaction: +1.31 V). $E┬░_{cell} = 0.34 - (-1.31) = +1.65$ V. Part (a)(ii): OH$^-$ ions migrate toward the anode (Zn electrode) through the electrolyte to complete the circuit ŌĆö anions migrate toward the anode in a galvanic cell.

parts.prompt:
A fresh zinc-air cell is weighed on an analytical balance before being placed in a hearing aid for use.
(i) As the cell operates, does the mass of the cell increase, decrease, or remain the same?
(ii) Justify your answer to part (b)(i) in terms of the equation for the overall cell reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): Increases. Part (b)(ii): The overall reaction 2 Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) ŌåÆ 2 ZnO(s) consumes O$_2$ from the surrounding air. This O$_2$ has mass that becomes incorporated into the ZnO product inside the cell, increasing the cell's total mass.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The mass increases. 1 point is earned for indicating an increase in cell mass.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct. Oxygen gas from the air enters the cell as a reactant and becomes incorporated into ZnO(s) inside the cell, adding mass.

sample_responses.response: The mass decreases because zinc is being consumed.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. While Zn is consumed, OŌéé from the air is incorporated into ZnO inside the cell. The net effect is a mass increase because the OŌéé mass is added to the cell.

alternatives.correct_example: Increases. O$_2$ from the surrounding air is incorporated into the cell as a reactant.

wrong_examples: Decreases or remains the same ŌĆö the cell gains the mass of O$_2$ consumed from outside air.
common_traps: The cell is open to air; O$_2$ enters from outside and becomes part of the ZnO product, adding mass.

scoring_points.description: For correct justification referencing O$_2$ from air entering the cell: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Oxygen gas from the air reacts with Zn(s) in the cell, producing ZnO(s), which has more mass than the original Zn(s). 1 point is earned for the justification.

sample_responses.response: According to the overall reaction $2\text{Zn}(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{ZnO}(s)$, molecular oxygen from the surrounding air is consumed and incorporated into the ZnO product inside the cell. The OŌéé enters from outside the cell, so the cell gains the mass of the oxygen consumed.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly references the overall reaction and explains that external OŌéé mass is added to the cell as ZnO is formed.

sample_responses.response: Zinc is being used up so the cell loses mass.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect direction. The Zn that reacts is already inside the cell, but the OŌéé reactant comes from outside. The net gain from OŌéé exceeds any interpretation of internal mass loss.

required_elements: The overall reaction 2 Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) ŌåÆ 2 ZnO(s) consumes O$_2$ gas from the surrounding air.
The mass of the O$_2$ from the air is added to the cell as ZnO.
alternatives.correct_example: According to 2 Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) ŌåÆ 2 ZnO(s), molecular oxygen from the atmosphere is consumed and incorporated into the zinc oxide product. The O$_2$ was external to the cell originally but becomes part of ZnO inside the cell, increasing the total mass.

wrong_examples: Saying the cell produces gas that escapes ŌĆö the reaction produces a solid (ZnO), which stays in the cell.
common_traps: The reaction product ZnO stays in the cell; the O$_2$ reactant comes from outside. Net result: the cell gains the mass of O$_2$.

parts.prompt:
The zinc-air cell is taken to the top of a mountain where the air pressure is lower.
(i) Will the cell potential be higher, lower, or the same as the cell potential at the lower elevation?
(ii) Justify your answer to part (c)(i) based on the equation for the overall cell reaction and the information
above.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): Lower. Part (c)(ii): At high altitude, partial pressure of O$_2$ is reduced. O$_2$ is a reactant in the cathode half-reaction. By the Nernst equation, lower P(O$_2$) increases Q and decreases the cell potential.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The cell potential will be lower. 1 point is earned for indicating a lower cell potential.

sample_responses.response: Lower. At higher altitude, the partial pressure of $O_2$ is reduced, decreasing the driving force for the cathode reaction.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct answer with correct reasoning: reduced P(OŌéé) at altitude ŌåÆ lower cell potential via Nernst equation.

sample_responses.response: Higher. Less air pressure means less resistance, so the cell operates more efficiently.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. Lower P(OŌéé) means less reactant for the cathode reaction, increasing Q and decreasing the cell potential.

alternatives.correct_example: Lower. At high altitude, the partial pressure of O$_2$ in the air is reduced.

wrong_examples: Higher or the same ŌĆö reduced O$_2$ pressure shifts the equilibrium away from products, lowering the cell potential.
common_traps: Lower P(O$_2$) ŌåÆ Q increases for the cell reaction ŌåÆ $E_{cell} < E┬░_{cell}$ (Nernst equation).

scoring_points.description: For justification referencing reduced O$_2$ partial pressure: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: O2(g), a reactant in the cell reaction, will be at a lower partial pressure at the higher elevation; thus the reaction has a greater value of Q (closer to K). Deviations in partial pressure that take the cell closer to equilibrium will decrease the magnitude of the cell potential. 1 point is earned for a justification that relates lower pressure (or concentration) of O2(g) to Q, or a qualitative approach using the Nernst equation.

sample_responses.response: At higher altitude, the atmospheric pressure is lower, so the partial pressure of $O_2$ is reduced. $O_2$ is a reactant in the overall cell reaction. By the Nernst equation, lower $P(O_2)$ increases Q (fewer reactants relative to products), which decreases the cell potential below $E┬░$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly links lower P(OŌéé) at altitude to a larger Q and smaller cell potential using the Nernst equation framework.

sample_responses.response: It is colder at altitude, so the reaction slows down, lowering the voltage.
sample_responses.commentary: Temperature is not the primary variable here. The question specifies lower air pressure. The correct argument uses reduced P(OŌéé) and the Nernst equation, not temperature effects.

required_elements: At higher elevation, the partial pressure of O$_2$ is reduced.
Lower P(O$_2$) decreases the driving force for the cathode reaction (less O$_2$ available for reduction), shifting Q and reducing $E_{cell}$.
alternatives.correct_example: At the mountaintop, atmospheric pressure is lower, so the partial pressure of O$_2$ is reduced. O$_2$ is a reactant in the overall reaction. By the Nernst equation, $E = E┬░ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$; lower P(O$_2$) increases Q (fewer reactants), which decreases the cell potential below $E┬░$.

wrong_examples: Referencing temperature ŌĆö the question specifies lower air pressure at altitude, not lower temperature.
common_traps: The cell reaction requires O$_2$ as a reactant. Any decrease in O$_2$ concentration/pressure reduces the thermodynamic driving force.

parts.prompt:
Metal-air cells need to be lightweight for many applications. In order to transfer more electrons with a
smaller mass, Na and Ca are investigated as potential anodes. A 1.0 g anode of which of these metals
would transfer more electrons, assuming that the anode is totally consumed during the lifetime of a cell?
Justify your answer with calculations.
parts.scoring_notes: Na (1 g): $n = 1.0/22.99 = 0.0435$ mol. Na$_2$O anode half-reaction: 2 Na ŌåÆ Na$_2$O + 2e$^-$ (or Na$_2$O + H$_2$O + 2e$^-$ ŌåÆ 2 Na + 2OH$^-$ reversed). Electrons per mol Na = 1 e$^-$/Na atom ŌåÆ 0.0435 mol e$^-$. Ca (1 g): $n = 1.0/40.08 = 0.0249$ mol. Ca ŌåÆ Ca$^{2+}$ + 2e$^-$, so 0.0499 mol e$^-$. Wait ŌĆö Na: 1 e$^-$ per atom, Ca: 2 e$^-$ per atom. Na: 0.0435 mol ├Ś 1 = 0.0435 mol e$^-$; Ca: 0.0249 mol ├Ś 2 = 0.0499 mol e$^-$. Ca transfers more electrons per gram.

scoring_points.description: For correct calculations comparing electrons transferred per gram for Na and Ca: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: For Na: 1.0 g Na ├Ś (1.0 mol Na / 22.99 g Na) ├Ś (1.0 mol eŌłÆ / 1.0 mol Na) = 0.043 mol eŌłÆ. For Ca: 1.0 g Ca ├Ś (1.0 mol Ca / 40.08 g Ca) ├Ś (2.0 mol eŌłÆ / 1.0 mol Ca) = 0.050 mol eŌłÆ. The cell with the Ca anode would transfer more electrons. 1 point for correct mole calculations; 1 point for taking 1 vs. 2 electrons into account and the correct answer.

sample_responses.response:
Na: $\frac{1.0\text{ g}}{22.99\text{ g/mol}} \times 1\text{ mol e}^-/\text{mol Na} = 0.043\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca: $\frac{1.0\text{ g}}{40.08\text{ g/mol}} \times 2\text{ mol e}^-/\text{mol Ca} = 0.050\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca transfers more electrons per gram.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly calculates moles of each metal per gram, accounts for 1 eŌü╗/Na vs. 2 eŌü╗/Ca from the oxidation states, and arrives at the correct conclusion.

sample_responses.response: Na: $\frac{1.0\text{ g}}{22.99\text{ g/mol}} = 0.043\text{ mol}$. Ca: $\frac{1.0\text{ g}}{40.08\text{ g/mol}} = 0.025\text{ mol}$. Na transfers more because it has more moles per gram.
sample_responses.commentary: Forgot to multiply by the number of electrons per atom (1 for Na, 2 for Ca). Ca's 2 electrons per atom compensates for its lower moles per gram, giving 0.050 mol eŌü╗ vs. 0.043 for Na.

required_elements: Na: $(1.0\text{ g}/22.99\text{ g/mol}) \times 1\text{ mol e}^-/\text{mol Na} = 0.043\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca: $(1.0\text{ g}/40.08\text{ g/mol}) \times 2\text{ mol e}^-/\text{mol Ca} = 0.050\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca transfers more electrons per gram of anode.
alternatives.correct_example:
Na (1 e$^-$ per atom): $1.0\text{ g} / 22.99\text{ g/mol} = 0.043\text{ mol Na} \times 1 = 0.043\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca (2 e$^-$ per atom): $1.0\text{ g} / 40.08\text{ g/mol} = 0.025\text{ mol Ca} \times 2 = 0.050\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca transfers more electrons per gram.

wrong_examples: Saying Na transfers more ŌĆö Na has a smaller molar mass but only 1 e$^-$ per atom; Ca has 2 e$^-$ per atom which compensates for its greater mass.
common_traps: Electrons transferred per gram = (1/M) ├Ś (e$^-$ per atom). Must account for both molar mass and oxidation state.

parts.prompt:
The only common oxide of zinc has the formula ZnO.
(i) Write the electron configuration for a Zn atom in the ground state.
(ii) From which sublevel are electrons removed when a Zn atom in the ground state is oxidized?
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): [Ar] 3d$^{10}$ 4s$^2$ (or full: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ 3s$^2$ 3p$^6$ 3d$^{10}$ 4s$^2$). Part (e)(ii): 4s. Electrons are removed from the highest-energy occupied subshell. For Zn, the 4s is higher in energy (outermost principal quantum number) than 3d, so the two 4s electrons are removed first when Zn is oxidized to Zn$^{2+}$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ground-state electron configuration of Zn: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ 3s$^2$ 3p$^6$ 4s$^2$ 3d$^{10}$ or [Ar] 4s$^2$ 3d$^{10}$. 1 point is earned for a correct configuration.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct noble-gas abbreviated configuration for Zn (Z = 30): argon core plus fully filled 3d and 4s subshells.
Incorrect. Zn fills 4s (not 4p) after the 3d subshell. The correct configuration ends in 3d┬╣Ōü░ 4s┬▓.

wrong_examples: [Ar] 4s$^2$ 3d$^9$ ŌĆö Zn has a completely filled 3d subshell.
common_traps: Zn (Z = 30) has a completely filled 3d$^{10}$ subshell plus 4s$^2$. Not to be confused with transition metals that have partially filled d subshells.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 4s sublevel. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.response: The 4s sublevel. When Zn ($[\text{Ar}]\, 3d^{10}\, 4s^2$) is oxidized to $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, the two electrons are removed from the 4s subshell, leaving $[\text{Ar}]\, 3d^{10}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the 4s sublevel as the source of the electrons removed during Zn oxidation.

sample_responses.response: The 3d sublevel, because 3d electrons are higher in energy than 4s.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. For d-block metals, the 4s electrons are removed first upon ionization, even though 3d is filled after 4s during aufbau. The 4s sublevel is removed first when Zn is oxidized.

alternatives.correct_example: The 4s sublevel. When Zn is oxidized to Zn$^{2+}$, both electrons are removed from the 4s subshell. The 4s electrons are in the highest principal energy level (n = 4) and are removed first.

wrong_examples: 3d ŌĆö while 3d is higher in energy than 4s for free atoms in some contexts, for transition metals and Zn the 4s electrons are removed first upon ionization.
common_traps: Electrons are removed in the reverse order of filling for ionization of d-block metals: 4s before 3d.
